query: roles of pyruvate carboxylase, PEP carboxykinase, pyruvate kinase, glucose-6-phosphatase in gluconeogenesis

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Gluconeogenesis -- Fundamentals -- Regulation of Gluconeogenesis
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple factors contribute to the regulation of substrates, enzymes, and reactions involved in gluconeogenesis, including: Glucagon regulates gluconeogenesis through [10] Changes in allosteric regulators (reduces the levels of fructose-2,6 bisphosphate) Covalent modification of enzyme activity (phosphorylation of pyruvate kinase results in its inactivation) Induction of enzymes gene expression (glucagon via CRE response elements increases the expression of PEPCK Acetyl CoA activates pyruvate carboxylase allosterically [11] Substrate availability might increase or decrease the rate of gluconeogenesis AMP inhibits fructose-1,6 bisphosphatase allosterically [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Insulin Metabolic Effects -- Function -- Role in Glycogen Metabolism
retrieved_docs.content: A variety of hepatic metabolic enzymes are under the direct control of insulin through gene transcription, which affects gene expression in metabolic pathways. In gluconeogenesis, insulin inhibits gene expression of the rate-limiting step, phosphoenolpyruvate carboxylase, fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase, and glucose-6-phosphatase. In glycolysis, gene expression of glucokinase and pyruvate kinase increases. Lipogenesis increases the expression of fatty acid synthase, pyruvate dehydrogenase, and acetyl-CoA carboxylase.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Gluconeogenesis -- Clinical Significance -- Pyruvate Carboxylase Deficiency
retrieved_docs.content: Pyruvate carboxylase deficiency is due to the lack of pyruvate carboxylase or altered enzyme activity. It causes lactic acidosis, hyperammonemia, and hypoglycemia. Hyperammonemia is due to pyruvate not being converted into oxaloacetic acid. Oxaloacetic acid gets transaminated into aspartate; reducing aspartate levels results in the reduced introduction of ammonia into the urea cycle. [27]

retrieved_docs.content: Phosphoglycerate mutase converts 2-phosphoglycerate to 3-phosphoglycerate.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gluconeogenesis -- Cellular Level -- Gluconeogenesis Reactions And Enzymes
retrieved_docs.content: In the mitochondrion, pyruvate carboxylase converts pyruvate to oxaloacetate. Pyruvate carboxylase requires ATP and the coenzyme biotin for activation. This conversion is the first step that reverses the nonequilibrium reaction catalyzed by the glycolytic enzyme pyruvate kinase.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gluconeogenesis -- Cellular Level -- Gluconeogenesis Reactions And Enzymes
retrieved_docs.content: Enolase hydrates PEP to form 2-phosphoglycerate.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gluconeogenesis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Gluconeogenesis essentially reverses glycolysis (see Image . Gluconeogenesis). Four enzymes facilitate glucose synthesis by this pathway by reversing 3 highly exergonic glycolytic steps, namely, pyruvate carboxylase, phosphoenol pyruvate carboxykinase (PEPCK), fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase, and glucose-6-phosphatase. However, these enzymes are not present in all cell types. Therefore, gluconeogenesis can only occur in specific tissues. In humans, gluconeogenesis takes place primarily in the liver and, to a lesser extent, the renal cortex. [2] This article discusses gluconeogenesis and its clinical correlates.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gluconeogenesis -- Cellular Level -- Gluconeogenesis Reactions And Enzymes
retrieved_docs.content: Phosphoglycerate kinase phosphorylates 3-phosphoglycerate to form 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate. This reaction requires ATP.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Gluconeogenesis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The following exergonic reaction catalyzed by PEP carboxykinase (PEPCK), a lyase, uses GTP as a phosphate donor to phosphorylate oxaloacetate and form PEP. Glucocorticoids induce PEPCK gene expression; cortisol, after binding to its steroid receptor intracellularly, moves inside the cell nucleus. Then, the zinc finger domain in cortisol binds to the glucocorticoid response element (GRE) on DNA. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Glucagon -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to promoting glycogenolysis, glucagon also inhibits glycogenesis in the liver at the same time. Glycogen synthase is a significant regulatory enzyme in the glycogenesis pathway. This enzyme catalyzes the transfer of a glucosyl residue from UDP-glucose to a nonreducing end of the branched glycogen molecule. Glucagon induces phosphorylation of glycogen synthase and thereby inhibits glycogen synthase activity in the liver. Phosphorylation of glycogen synthase by multiple kinases, including PKA and other serine/threonine kinases leading to a graded inactivation, which leads to decreased glycogen synthesis and therefore increases the glucose output to the blood. [8] [23] Glucagon-induced phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase (PEPCK) is the enzyme in a rate-limiting step in hepatic gluconeogenesis. PKA activation leads to the activation of CREB, leading to the increased synthesis of PGC-1 protein. Both PGC-1 and HNF-4 are coactivators that increase the transcription of the PEPCK gene. In addition to the above functions, glucagon inhibits glycolysis by inhibiting phosphofructokinase-1. Glucagon also stimulates lipolysis and ketogenesis, and the ratio of glucagon to insulin perceived by the liver is a critical signal for the liver to convert fatty acids to ketone bodies as in diabetic hyperglycemic coma. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gluconeogenesis -- Cellular Level -- Gluconeogenesis Reactions And Enzymes
retrieved_docs.content: Aldolase combines glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate and DHAP to form fructose 1,6-bisphosphate.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gluconeogenesis -- Cellular Level -- Gluconeogenesis Reactions And Enzymes
retrieved_docs.content: In the cytosol, PEPCK decarboxylates oxaloacetate, which then rearranges to form phosphoenol pyruvate (PEP). PEPCK requires GTP and magnesium ions for activation. This transformation is the second step that reverses the nonequilibrium reaction catalyzed by pyruvate kinase.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2773
retrieved_docs.content: Erythropetin and glukagon are both members of the broad class “Amino Acid, Peptide, or Protein.” As peptide hormones, they share the structural characteristic of being protein‑based signaling molecules, yet they serve very different physiological functions. Erythropetin regulates erythropoiesis by stimulating the production of red blood cells, while glukagon raises blood glucose levels by promoting glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis. Recognizing that these distinct hormones belong to the same molecular category highlights their common biochemical nature and informs both basic understanding of hormone biology and the development of protein‑based therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogenolytic hormones are a class of hormones that stimulate the breakdown of glycogen into glucose, thereby increasing blood glucose levels. Pancreatic hormones, which are secreted by the pancreas, fall within this category because they include glucagon, a key glycogenolytic hormone that promotes glycogenolysis. This relationship highlights the role of pancreatic endocrine function in regulating glucose metabolism through glycogenolytic activity.

Finally, trisaccharides, as short carbohydrate chains, can serve as substrates or regulatory sugars that modulate glycosylation and other carbohydrate‑based biosynthetic processes. Together, these entities illustrate the wide range of chemical, pharmacologic, and biological factors that can define, regulate, or modify biosynthetic mechanisms, underscoring their importance for understanding metabolic regulation and for developing therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1977
retrieved_docs.content: Klofibrát produces a physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, thereby influencing lipid metabolism, but it is contraindicated in patients with تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic steatosis). Consequently, clinicians should consider the lipid‑lowering benefit of Klofibrát while avoiding its use in individuals with hepatic steatosis to prevent potential liver‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2404
retrieved_docs.content: Metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) is a broad category of disease that includes carbohydrate metabolism disorder (اضطراب استقلاب الكربوهيدرات) as one of its subtypes. Within the hierarchy of carbohydrate metabolism disorder, other endocrine disorders are classified as a further subset, indicating that these endocrine conditions are considered specific forms of carbohydrate metabolism disorders. This hierarchical relationship highlights how metabolic disorders encompass a range of more specialized conditions, including those affecting carbohydrate processing and related endocrine functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1603
retrieved_docs.content: The endokrinní systém (endocrine system) is the physiological network that regulates numerous metabolic processes and includes conditions such as متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome) as a manifestation of its dysregulation. Within this system, pankreatické hormony (pancreatic hormones) represent a specific group of secretions that contribute to glucose homeostasis and other endocrine functions. Medical interventions that affect hormonal balance, such as kastrace (castration), are procedures that target the endokrinní systém, reflecting its role as a therapeutic focus in certain clinical contexts. Glycogenolytic hormones, which promote the breakdown of glycogen to glucose, constitute a distinct class of hormones that are not classified as pankreatické hormony, underscoring the diversity of hormonal actions within and beyond the endocrine system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 223
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin aspart, a human insulin analog, exerts its pharmacologic action by targeting the human insulin receptor (INSR protein) and produces a physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis. Klofibrát, a fibrate‑type lipid‑lowering agent, also leads to decreased lipolysis as a physiologic effect. Thus, both insulin aspart and klofibrát share the outcome of reduced lipolysis, although insulin aspart achieves this through direct activation of the INSR protein, while klofibrát acts via its own distinct metabolic pathway. Recognizing this common effect can inform therapeutic decisions when managing conditions related to lipid metabolism and glucose regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1341
retrieved_docs.content: Beta‑D‑glucopyranose is classified as a saccharide, belonging to the broad group of carbohydrate molecules known as saccharidy. As a specific sugar monomer, beta‑D‑glucopyranose exemplifies the structural and functional characteristics of saccharides, which serve as essential energy sources, metabolic intermediates, and structural components in biological systems. Recognizing beta‑D‑glucopyranose as a member of the saccharidy class aids in understanding its role in nutrition, biochemical pathways, and laboratory analyses of carbohydrate content.

Additionally, castration (kastrace) is identified as a procedure that targets the endocrine system. Clinically, the condition “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium” has its findings located in the endocrine system, underscoring the endocrine system’s central role in gestational glucose‑metabolism disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 308
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin aspart, human, is a rapid‑acting insulin analog whose primary physiologic effect is the reduction of Decreased Lipolysis. This effect is mediated through its binding to the INSR protein, human, which serves as the molecular target of insulin aspart. By interacting with the human insulin receptor, insulin aspart suppresses the breakdown of triglycerides in adipose tissue, contributing to improved glucose homeostasis and overall metabolic regulation. This connection highlights the central role of the insulin receptor in mediating the lipolysis‑inhibiting action of insulin aspart in clinical practice.

Metabolic disorders of lipoproteins and other lipidemias are a subcategory of disorders of lipid metabolism, inversely linked (ISA) to the Arabic term اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي (metabolic disorder) and classified as اضطراب استقلاب الدهون (disorder of fat metabolism). This group maps to type IIa hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ) and includes early‑onset diabetes/hyperglycemia (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد), demonstrating clinical overlap between dyslipidemia and glucose‑regulation disorders. Autosomal dominant inheritance is the pattern for type IIa hyperlipoproteinemia, and the lipid‑modifying drug class fibrates (e.g., bezafibrát, clofibrate, fenofibrate) is used to treat these disorders; clofibrate is contraindicated in liver cirrhosis. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1880
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism constitute a broad category of metabolic conditions affecting the processing and regulation of fats in the body, and hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) is a specific disorder within this category. Hyperlipoproteinemia is characterized by abnormally high levels of lipoproteins in the blood, reflecting a particular manifestation of the underlying disturbances in lipid metabolism. Recognizing hyperlipoproteinemia as a subset of disorders of lipid metabolism underscores its role in the spectrum of lipid‑related diseases and informs targeted diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.
